caseStudies.title: Sharp Marketing Agency (SMA) — October Transactions
caseStudies.text:
Sharp Marketing Agency (SMA) operates a small digital marketing firm. In October, the company recorded the following transactions:

a. Provided marketing services for clients, earning $50,000. Of this amount, $30,000 was collected in cash, and the remaining $20,000 will be collected next month.

b. Incurred salary expense of $35,000, which was paid in full in cash.

c. Purchased $4,000 in office supplies. The company paid $1,500 in cash, with the remaining $2,500 placed on account, payable within 30 days.

d. Paid $150 to repair the office coffee machine, which broke down suddenly.

e. Paid a freelance graphic designer $1,500 for work completed last month.

f. Received a special pre-payment from a customer for a project to be completed in the upcoming 3 months. The customer paid $6,000 in full. The project is estimated to cost the company $3,000 in materials.

g. Incurred depreciation of equipment and furniture of $2,500 for the month.

h. Incurred a loss of $1,200 when an uninsured laptop was stolen during an office break-in.

i. Determined after a physical count at the end of the month that the company had $500 of office supplies remaining. This ending balance represents a $300 increase from the beginning balance of the month.

caseStudies.title: BeverageCo — Financial Ratio Analysis
caseStudies.text:
BeverageCo is a mid-sized beverage manufacturing company that produces a range of bottled drinks for both domestic and regional markets. The company operates in an intensely competitive industry where shifting consumer preferences and rising health awareness are reshaping market dynamics. Management is undertaking a comprehensive evaluation of financial health against industry benchmarks.

Ratio Data:

| Ratio                        | 2023  | 2022  | 2021  | 2020  | 2019  | Industry 2023 | Industry 5-yr avg |
|------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|---------------|-------------------|
| Current Ratio                |  1.80 |  1.70 |  1.62 |  1.55 |  1.40 |          1.30 |              1.35 |
| Quick Ratio                  |  0.50 |  0.55 |  0.48 |  0.45 |  0.70 |          0.80 |              0.82 |
| Inventory Turnover (times)   |  3.50 |  3.80 |  3.50 |  3.00 |  5.00 |          6.00 |              5.80 |
| Days' Purchases Outstanding  |    75 |    70 |    80 |    85 |    60 |            65 |                63 |
| Gross Margin (%)             | 45.0% | 46.0% | 43.0% | 42.0% | 45.0% |         42.0% |             41.0% |
| Profit Margin (%)            |  3.0% |  4.0% |  1.8% |  1.2% |  6.0% |          7.0% |              7.5% |
| Debt Ratio (%)               | 50.0% | 49.0% | 48.0% | 50.0% | 35.0% |         40.0% |             38.0% |
| Interest Cover Ratio (times) |  2.50 |  3.00 |  1.70 |  1.30 |  5.00 |          4.50 |              4.80 |

caseStudies.title: Sweet Confectionery Ltd — Sell or Process Further Decision
caseStudies.text:
Sweet Confectionery Ltd (SC) currently produces 5,000 raw chocolate bars per month, which are sold to distributors for a total monthly revenue of $12,500. The company is now considering further processing these bars into chocolate-covered pretzels. Relevant operation data:

1. Monthly production of raw chocolate bars: 5,000 units
2. Selling price of raw chocolate bars: $2.50 per unit
3. Total fixed production costs for raw chocolate bars: $8,000 per month
4. Monthly production of pretzels: 6,000 units (5,000 raw bars yield 6,000 pretzels due to additional inputs)
5. Selling price of chocolate-covered pretzels: $4.00 per unit
6. Additional materials cost for pretzel production: $0.20 per pretzel
7. Processing cost to convert each bar into a pretzel: $1.00 per pretzel
8. Additional monthly fixed overhead for pretzel production: $3,000
9. The processing facility could be used for an alternative project generating monthly profit of $1,500
10. All produced pretzels can be sold

caseStudies.title: Apparel Ltd — Cash Budget Q1 20X6
caseStudies.text:
Apparel Ltd is a mid-sized retail company specialising in casual clothing for young adults, operating three outlets and an online store. The finance team is developing a cash budget for Q1 20X6 (January–March).

Sales and Collections:
  Monthly sales: January $200,000 | February $250,000 | March $300,000 | April $320,000
  Selling price: $100 per unit
  60% of sales collected in month of sale; 40% collected in the following month
  Beginning accounts receivable (1 January): $76,000

Purchases and Payments:
  Inventory purchased = 60% of next month's expected unit sales, at $30 per unit
  80% of purchases paid in month of purchase; 20% paid in the following month
  Beginning accounts payable (1 January): $11,760

Operating Expenses:
  Fixed monthly expenses: $30,000 (of which 30% is depreciation/amortisation — non-cash)
  Variable operating expenses (marketing, delivery, commissions): 15% of monthly sales, paid in month incurred

Additional:
  Beginning cash balance (1 January 20X6): $20,000

questions.label: Question 1a
questions.text:
For each transaction (a) to (i), explain its impact on SMA's accounts in the expanded accounting equation (Assets, Liabilities, Equity, Revenues, Expenses) in October.

List each account affected separately. Use this format:
  Item | Account Type | Amount ($) | Increase or Decrease

Note for item (i): consider the beginning balance of supplies and total supplies available before calculating supplies consumed.
questions.modelAnswer:
a. Marketing services $50,000 ($30k cash + $20k receivable):
   Assets (Cash) | $30,000 | Increase
   Assets (Accounts Receivable) | $20,000 | Increase
   Revenue (Service Revenue) | $50,000 | Increase

b. Salaries $35,000 paid in cash:
   Expenses (Salary Expense) | $35,000 | Increase
   Assets (Cash) | $35,000 | Decrease

c. Office supplies $4,000 ($1,500 cash + $2,500 on account):
   Assets (Office Supplies) | $4,000 | Increase
   Assets (Cash) | $1,500 | Decrease
   Liabilities (Accounts Payable) | $2,500 | Increase

d. Coffee machine repair $150 cash:
   Expenses (Repair Expense) | $150 | Increase
   Assets (Cash) | $150 | Decrease

e. Freelancer paid $1,500 for last month's work:
   Expenses (Contract/Freelance Expense) | $1,500 | Increase
   Assets (Cash) | $1,500 | Decrease

f. Customer pre-payment $6,000 for 3-month project:
   Assets (Cash) | $6,000 | Increase
   Liabilities (Unearned Revenue) | $6,000 | Increase
   [The $3,000 project cost is a future cost — not recorded now]

g. Depreciation of equipment and furniture $2,500:
   Expenses (Depreciation Expense) | $2,500 | Increase
   Assets (Accumulated Depreciation) | $2,500 | Increase

h. Loss from stolen laptop $1,200 (uninsured):
   Expenses (Loss on Theft) | $1,200 | Increase
   Assets (Equipment) | $1,200 | Decrease

i. Office supplies consumed:
   Beginning supplies balance = $500 (ending) – $300 (increase) = $200
   Total available = $200 (beginning) + $4,000 (purchased, item c) = $4,200
   Supplies used = $4,200 – $500 (ending) = $3,700
   Expenses (Office Supplies Expense) | $3,700 | Increase
   Assets (Office Supplies) | $3,700 | Decrease
keyPoints: Item (a): three accounts — Cash $30k, AR $20k, Revenue $50k (NOT all cash)
Item (b): Salary expense, Cash decrease
Item (c): Office Supplies increase $4,000; Cash decrease $1,500; AP increase $2,500
Item (d): Repair expense $150, Cash decrease
Item (e): Expense $1,500, Cash decrease — records the payment for prior month work
Item (f): Cash increase $6,000, Unearned Revenue (Liability) increase $6,000 — NOT revenue yet
Item (g): Depreciation expense, Accumulated Depreciation increase (Assets decrease)
Item (h): Loss expense $1,200, Asset (Equipment) decreases $1,200
Item (i): Must work out supplies used: beginning $200 + purchased $4,000 – ending $500 = $3,700

questions.label: Question 1b
questions.text: Calculate the net profit or loss for SMA in October based on the transactions listed above.
questions.modelAnswer:
Revenue:
  Service Revenue                        $50,000

Expenses:
  Salary Expense                         $35,000
  Repair Expense                            $150
  Contract/Freelance Expense              $1,500
  Depreciation Expense                    $2,500
  Loss on Theft                           $1,200
  Office Supplies Expense                 $3,700
  Total Expenses                         $44,050

Net Profit = $50,000 – $44,050 = $5,950

Note: The $6,000 customer pre-payment (item f) is Unearned Revenue — not included in October revenue as the project has not yet been performed.
keyPoints: Revenue = $50,000 (service revenue only)
6 expense items: salary $35,000, repair $150, freelancer $1,500, depreciation $2,500, theft loss $1,200, supplies $3,700
Total expenses = $44,050
Net profit = $5,950
The $6,000 pre-payment must NOT be included as revenue

questions.label: Question 1c
questions.text: Assess the company's performance in October. Assume total assets at end of October are $76,000 and the required rate of return is 10%.
questions.modelAnswer:
Return on Assets (ROA) = Net Profit After Tax ÷ Total Assets
= $5,950 ÷ $76,000
= 7.83%

Required rate of return = 10%

Assessment: SMA's ROA of 7.83% falls below the required rate of return of 10%. For every $1 of assets deployed, the company generated only $0.0783 of profit, against a minimum expectation of $0.10.

This suggests the company's assets are not generating sufficient returns in October. However, several expense items are one-off or non-cash (laptop theft loss $1,200, depreciation $2,500), which suppressed October's profit. Excluding the theft loss (a non-recurring event), adjusted profit would be $7,150, giving an ROA of 9.41% — closer to but still below the 10% target.

Management should focus on revenue growth or cost optimisation to meet the required return on assets.
keyPoints: Formula: ROA = Net Profit ÷ Total Assets = $5,950 ÷ $76,000 = 7.83%
Compare to required rate 10%: below target
Must state: performance is inadequate — not meeting required return
Good answers discuss non-recurring items (theft) that distorted October profit
Recommendation to improve ROA

questions.label: Question 2
questions.text: Analyse the ratios provided for BeverageCo and comment on its profitability, liquidity, solvency, and efficiency. A friend is considering investing in this industry. Using only the information provided, would you recommend buying shares of BeverageCo? Justify your recommendation.
questions.modelAnswer:
PROFITABILITY:
Gross margin improved slightly from 45% (2019) to 45% (2023), remaining above industry (42%), suggesting good cost-of-goods management. However, profit margin has collapsed from 6% (2019) to 3% (2023), far below the industry average of 7% and 5-year industry average of 7.5%. This widening gap between gross margin and profit margin indicates rising operating and/or finance expenses are severely eroding net profitability. Despite reasonable top-line cost control, the company is not converting revenue into bottom-line profit efficiently.

LIQUIDITY:
The current ratio has improved from 1.40 (2019) to 1.80 (2023), now above the industry average (1.30) — a positive sign for short-term debt coverage. However, the quick ratio has deteriorated from 0.70 (2019) to 0.50 (2023), well below the industry average of 0.80. The large gap between current (1.80) and quick (0.50) ratios signals excessive inventory build-up. BeverageCo cannot meet short-term liabilities without relying heavily on inventory liquidation — a risk in a rapidly changing consumer market (health-conscious shift reducing demand for traditional beverages).

SOLVENCY / GEARING:
The debt ratio has worsened significantly from 35% (2019) to 50% (2023), now 10 percentage points above the industry average of 40%. This means half of the company's assets are funded by debt. More critically, interest cover has fallen from 5.0× to 2.5×, far below the industry average of 4.5×. At 2.5×, the company has little buffer to absorb earnings declines before it cannot service its debt — this is a serious red flag. The company's financial risk has materially increased.

EFFICIENCY:
Inventory turnover has declined sharply from 5.0× (2019) to 3.5× (2023), well below the industry average of 6.0×. This confirms slow-moving inventory, consistent with the liquidity concerns above. Days' Purchases Outstanding has increased from 60 days to 75 days (vs. industry 65 days), suggesting the company is delaying supplier payments — possibly due to cash pressure from poor profitability.

INVESTMENT RECOMMENDATION: Do NOT recommend buying BeverageCo shares.
Despite superficially improving liquidity (current ratio) and gross margins, the fundamental picture is deteriorating: profit margin is 3% vs. industry 7% and declining; interest cover of 2.5× creates significant default risk; debt ratio at 50% leaves little financial flexibility; and inventory turnover far below industry average indicates operational inefficiency. The company has not capitalised on the health-beverage trend, leaving a strategic gap. These combined risks make BeverageCo a poor investment at this time.
keyPoints: Profitability: Gross margin above industry — good; Profit margin 3% vs industry 7% — very poor; declining trend since 2019
Liquidity: Current ratio improving and above industry — positive; Quick ratio 0.50 vs industry 0.80 — concerning; large gap = heavy inventory reliance
Solvency: Debt ratio 50% vs industry 40% — high leverage; Interest cover 2.5× vs industry 4.5× — dangerous, little debt-service buffer
Efficiency: Inventory turnover 3.5× vs industry 6.0× — slow moving; DPO 75 days vs industry 65 days — stretching suppliers
Investment recommendation: NOT recommended — must give clear recommendation with minimum 2–3 reasons linked to ratios
Must discuss all 4 dimensions: profitability, liquidity, solvency, efficiency

questions.label: Question 3a
questions.text: List the relevant revenues and costs that should be considered when deciding whether Sweet Confectionery Ltd should sell the raw chocolate bars or process them into pretzels. Briefly explain why each item is relevant or irrelevant.
questions.modelAnswer:
RELEVANT REVENUES:

1. Revenue from selling raw chocolate bars: $2.50 × 5,000 = $12,500/month
   → RELEVANT. This is the revenue FOREGONE if the company processes further. It represents the opportunity cost of not selling raw bars. Relevant because it differs between the two alternatives.

2. Revenue from selling pretzels: $4.00 × 6,000 = $24,000/month
   → RELEVANT. This is the NEW revenue earned only if the company processes further. It differs between alternatives.

RELEVANT COSTS (incremental — arise only if processing occurs):

3. Additional materials cost: $0.20 × 6,000 = $1,200/month
   → RELEVANT. This cost is incurred ONLY if processing occurs. It is a future cost that differs between alternatives.

4. Processing cost (converting bars to pretzels): $1.00 × 6,000 = $6,000/month
   → RELEVANT. Only incurred if the decision to process is made. Incremental future cost.

5. Additional fixed overhead for pretzel production: $3,000/month
   → RELEVANT. This is an additional fixed cost specifically for pretzel production, incurred only if the company processes further. It is avoidable if the company does not process.

6. Opportunity cost of processing facility: $1,500/month
   → RELEVANT. If the processing facility is used for pretzels, the company forgoes $1,500 profit from the alternative project. Opportunity costs represent the value of the best alternative forgone — relevant to the decision.

IRRELEVANT COSTS:

7. Fixed production costs for raw chocolate bars: $8,000/month
   → IRRELEVANT. These costs are incurred regardless of whether the company sells raw bars or processes them into pretzels. They are sunk/committed costs that do not differ between alternatives. Including them would not change the decision.
keyPoints: Relevant revenues: raw bar revenue (foregone) $12,500 AND pretzel revenue $24,000 — both relevant as they differ between alternatives
Relevant costs: additional materials $1,200 + processing cost $6,000 + additional fixed overhead $3,000 + opportunity cost $1,500
IRRELEVANT: fixed production costs for raw bars $8,000 — sunk/unavoidable cost, incurred regardless of decision
Must explain WHY each item is relevant (future, differs between alternatives) or irrelevant (sunk, does not change)
Opportunity cost must be included and explained as profit foregone from alternative use of facility

questions.label: Question 3b
questions.text: Using the relevant revenues and costs identified in Question 3a, evaluate whether Sweet Confectionery Ltd should continue selling the raw chocolate bars or process them further into pretzels. Provide supporting calculations.
questions.modelAnswer:
Incremental Analysis — Process Further vs Sell Raw:

                                          $
Revenue from pretzels (6,000 × $4.00)   24,000
Less: Revenue from raw bars (foregone)  (12,500)
Incremental Revenue                      11,500

Less Incremental Costs:
  Additional materials ($0.20 × 6,000)   (1,200)
  Processing cost ($1.00 × 6,000)        (6,000)
  Additional fixed overhead              (3,000)
  Opportunity cost (alternative project) (1,500)
Total Incremental Costs                 (11,700)

Net Benefit/(Loss) of Processing          (200)

Decision: Sweet Confectionery Ltd should NOT process further into pretzels. The incremental costs ($11,700) exceed the incremental revenue gain ($11,500) by $200 per month, resulting in a marginal net loss. The company is marginally better off continuing to sell raw chocolate bars.

Note: The margin is very small ($200/month). Any slight increase in pretzel selling price or reduction in processing costs could reverse this decision.
keyPoints: Incremental revenue = $24,000 – $12,500 = $11,500
Net loss from processing = $200
Decision: Do NOT process further
Fixed production cost $8,000 must NOT appear in the calculation (irrelevant)

questions.label: Question 3c
questions.text: Identify any other factors the company should consider before making the final decision.
questions.modelAnswer:
1. Market demand uncertainty: The calculation assumes all 6,000 pretzels can be sold at $4.00 each. If actual demand is lower, revenue will fall and the net loss widens. The company should assess market demand for pretzels before committing.

2. Quality and brand risk: Processing raw bars into pretzels is a different product. If quality is inconsistent or the product does not align with the brand image, it could damage the company's reputation and existing customer relationships with distributors.

3. One-time setup and transition costs: The analysis captures ongoing monthly costs but ignores any one-time capital expenditure for equipment setup, staff training, or production line modifications — these could make the decision even less favourable initially.

4. Long-term strategic fit: Even though the monthly financial difference is only $200, the pretzel market may have higher growth potential. Conversely, the existing distributor relationships for raw bars may be strategically valuable and difficult to rebuild if abandoned.
keyPoints: Market demand risk — assumption that all pretzels sell at $4.00 may not hold
Brand/quality risk — new product may not align with existing brand or relationships
One-time setup costs not captured in the analysis
Strategic/long-term considerations (growth potential, customer relationships)

questions.label: Question 4a
questions.text:
Present a cash budget for Apparel Ltd for Q1 20X6 (January, February, March).

Your budget must clearly show:
- Cash receipts: collections from sales (current + prior month AR)
- Cash payments: purchases (current + prior month AP) and operating expenses
- Opening and closing cash balances for each month
questions.modelAnswer:
STEP 1 — Units sold each month:
  Jan: $200,000 ÷ $100 = 2,000 units
  Feb: $250,000 ÷ $100 = 2,500 units
  Mar: $300,000 ÷ $100 = 3,000 units
  Apr: $320,000 ÷ $100 = 3,200 units

STEP 2 — Purchases (60% of NEXT month's units × $30):
  Jan purchases: 60% × 2,500 × $30 = 1,500 × $30 = $45,000
  Feb purchases: 60% × 3,000 × $30 = 1,800 × $30 = $54,000
  Mar purchases: 60% × 3,200 × $30 = 1,920 × $30 = $57,600
  (Dec purchases derived from opening AP: $11,760 ÷ 20% = $58,800)

STEP 3 — Cash collections (60% in month of sale, 40% next month):
  January:
    Current month (60% × $200k)          $120,000
    Prior month AR (opening balance)       $76,000
    Total                                $196,000
  February:
    Current month (60% × $250k)          $150,000
    Prior month (40% × $200k)             $80,000
    Total                                $230,000
  March:
    Current month (60% × $300k)          $180,000
    Prior month (40% × $250k)            $100,000
    Total                                $280,000

STEP 4 — Cash payments for purchases (80% in purchase month, 20% next month):
  January:
    Current (80% × $45,000)              $36,000
    Prior month AP (opening balance)     $11,760
    Total                               $47,760
  February:
    Current (80% × $54,000)             $43,200
    Prior month (20% × $45,000)          $9,000
    Total                               $52,200
  March:
    Current (80% × $57,600)             $46,080
    Prior month (20% × $54,000)         $10,800
    Total                               $56,880

STEP 5 — Operating expenses:
  Fixed cash expenses = $30,000 × 70% = $21,000/month [30% = D&A, non-cash]
  Variable (15% of sales):
    Jan: 15% × $200,000 = $30,000  |  Feb: 15% × $250,000 = $37,500  |  Mar: 15% × $300,000 = $45,000
  Total cash opex:
    Jan: $21,000 + $30,000 = $51,000
    Feb: $21,000 + $37,500 = $58,500
    Mar: $21,000 + $45,000 = $66,000

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CASH BUDGET — Apparel Ltd, Q1 20X6
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
                                Jan          Feb          Mar
Opening Cash Balance         $20,000     $117,240     $236,540

ADD: Cash Receipts
  Collections (current 60%)  $120,000     $150,000     $180,000
  Collections (prior 40%/AR)  $76,000      $80,000     $100,000
Total Receipts               $196,000     $230,000     $280,000

LESS: Cash Payments
  Purchases (current 80%)     $36,000      $43,200      $46,080
  Purchases (prior 20%/AP)    $11,760       $9,000      $10,800
  Operating expenses          $51,000      $58,500      $66,000
Total Payments               $98,760     $110,700     $122,880

Closing Cash Balance        $117,240     $236,540     $393,660
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
keyPoints: Jan units 2,000 | Feb 2,500 | Mar 3,000 | Apr 3,200
Jan collections: $120,000 (current) + $76,000 (opening AR) = $196,000
Jan purchase payments: $36,000 (current 80%) + $11,760 (opening AP) = $47,760
Fixed cash opex = $21,000/month (70% of $30,000 — exclude 30% D&A)
Closing balances: Jan $117,240 | Feb $236,540 | Mar $393,660

questions.label: Question 4b
questions.text: Evaluate Apparel Ltd's cash status based on the cash budget prepared in Question 4a.
questions.modelAnswer:
Apparel Ltd is in a strong and improving cash position throughout Q1 20X6.

The cash balance grows consistently from $20,000 (opening) to $117,240 (end of January), $236,540 (end of February), and $393,660 (end of March). There is no cash shortage at any point — the company maintains positive cash balances in every month without any need for external financing.

The strong cash generation is driven by robust collections (60% same-month collection provides reliable inflows) and growing sales volumes. Cash receipts exceed payments in every month, producing a net inflow of approximately $97,240 in January, $119,300 in February, and $157,120 in March.

However, the rapidly accumulating cash balance ($393,660 by March) suggests the company may be holding excessive idle cash. Management should consider deploying the surplus productively — for example, paying down any existing debt early, investing in short-term securities, funding inventory expansion ahead of expected sales growth, or returning value to shareholders.

Overall, the cash budget presents a healthy picture of cash management for Q1 20X6, with no financing concerns identified.
keyPoints: Cash position is positive and growing every month — no shortage
Closing balances: Jan $117,240 | Feb $236,540 | Mar $393,660
No external financing required
Must note the growing surplus — suggest productive deployment (invest, repay debt, etc.)
Overall assessment: healthy cash management
